evidence_quotes.quote: protect consumer access to purchased video games, particularly when publishers shut down official servers or revoke access unilaterally.
turned a one-time purchase into a revocable license
This leaves consumers with no access, no recourse, and no compensation.

paragraphs.text: As part of the Digital Fairness Act, I respectfully urge the European Commission to include provisions that protect consumer access to purchased video games, particularly when publishers shut down official servers or revoke access unilaterally. Today's video games are frequently sold without clear end-of-life disclosures, yet publishers retain the right to render them unusable post-sale. This leaves consumers with no access, no recourse, and no compensation. Practices like these have effectively turned a one-time purchase into a revocable license, eroding the fundamental concept of digital ownership. For many buyers, a game is not just entertainment it is a meaningful investment, often tied to personal, emotional, or artistic value. Recent cases, such as the deactivation of The Crew or the uncertain future of titles like Titanfall 2, illustrate how entire digital products are being killed despite being paid for by consumers in good faith. These are not just technical decisions they are violations of trust and a clear example of unfair digital commercial practices. Video games are increasingly recognized as cultural works of art, not disposable services. Their preservation and accessibility should be treated with the same dignity as other digital or creative media. At the very least, a consumer who purchases a game should be confident they can still use it after official support ends without legal ambiguity or fear of license revocation. Including these provisions in the Digital Fairness Act would reaffirm the EU's commitment to consumer rights, cultural preservation, and ethical digital commerce. It would also restore faith in the digital marketplace, where consumers should be empowered not exploited by the technology they embrace.
